speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. It's January 22, 1996. Interview is with Joe Dispeker. Dispeker, last name at birth. Interview by Judy Cowan. It's Los Angeles, California, USA, speaking English. Tape Number 1. I'm Judy Cowan. This morning I will be interviewing Joe Dispeker in Los Angeles, California. The date is January 22, 1996. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you state your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joe Dispeker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Fuerth in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 87 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a Sigmund Dispeker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was Elsbeth Dispeker nee Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never did. I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember only one grandmother personally. The others died before-- I was either too young, or they died before I was born. But my paternal grandmother I remember very well. She was a very good friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Laura Dispeker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about your very earliest recollections in Fuerth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- my very earliest recollection is that I had a very happy youth. We were in good financial conditions. And my parents were wonderful people. And I had nice friends. And the whole world looked great when I was young. I can only look back at my childhood as the most wonderful time of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant. He was in the woolen business that was a family business for 100 years. In fact, we had our 100th anniversary just one year before Hitler came to power and it all ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was it that started the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was started by my great-grandfather, Isaak Dispeker, who came to Fuerth, from a small town, near Fuerth called Baiersdorf, where Jews lived since the 16th century or even before. And he started a woolen business in 1832.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he come to Fuerth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was still subject to the [? matricula ?] rule, which meant that there was a strict quota for Jews to be allowed to live in the city or in towns that were bigger than the open country. And some-- one Jew had to die for another one to come in and get the document, which gave him a number. And he was always Jew number so and so, whatever the number was. And this lasted until 1848, when the [? matricula ?] was finally abolished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took someone's place who died and that's how he was able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how he was able to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your family lived in Fuerth [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up until the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the holidays were-- the high holy days were strictly observed in the family. We kept our fairly large business closed on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. And the family was-- celebrated in the traditional way, in which we were used to celebrate for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider your upbringing traditional? Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say traditional-- traditional to reformed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a general modernization trend amongst the German Jews in those days. And they were no longer Orthodox. But they knew that there were Jews. And they were acting and feeling as Jews all along.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, do you remember assimilating or playing with Christian children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. In fact, the first time in my life that I found out that I-- that I knew consciously that I was Jewish was when I went to a boys school in which my teacher was a man by the name of Louis Kissinger, who was the father of the Secretary of State. And he was a teacher in that school. And in Germany religious instruction was given by the schools. In other words, it was obligatory that it was just like math or reading. Religion was a regular school-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Subject.

speaker: interviewee
text: Subject. And he took the six Jewish boys that were in the class-- six or seven-- out and brought them into another classroom. And he called our names. And we didn't know why he called our names. At least I didn't know. And then I came home, and I told my father what happened. And he explained to me that we were a different religion than the other people. Fuerth was a town in which Jews and Christians got along famously. Some of the best friends of my parents and grandparents were Gentiles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It all ended by force after 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Tell me a little bit more about your education. After you went to that school--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to boys' school for four years. That was during the years of World War I, in which the school was manned by two teachers only. Everybody else was at war. And in 1919-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1919-- I entered the gymnasium, which was a nine year school that's preparatory to the university. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there-- also, there were quite a few Jewish boys in the gymnasium. And we were good friends with all of them, with all kids in school and all the boys. And later on, we were practically grownups when we left the school. And we were all good friends. In fact, we belonged to the same fraternity. We had a fraternity in the last year, which was called Abituria--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1928. And even at that time we were just Jews and Gentiles together singing student songs and in a way playing Alt Heidelberg, in our way-- drinking beer and having a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any incidents of anti-Semitism that you remember from those happy times?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some, but they were minor. There were some remarks where some teachers made perhaps remarks about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anti-Semitic remarks of some kind or another. And they elicited protests by the parents. Usually ended in apology. And there was some time-- there was only actually one ridiculous incident that, while all the Jewish boys were out on Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur-- I don't know what it was. The other kids said, we're playing a trick on them. When they come back, we won't talk to them, because they were a little bit envious that it was that the Jewish boys had the days off. But that was all straightened out. It was really-- we might have taken it serious at the time. But it was not serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think things began to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things began to change in the years before-- actually in the years-- in the early 1930s, Hitler came to power in 1933. And from 1930 on, it changed, because by then, many of the boys and young men had entered the Hitler guards. And it was very attractive to them-- beautiful uniforms and parades and all this kind of stuff. And they were indoctrinated with that violent anti-Semitism. And then, of course, after 1933, and after the Nazis took over, the radio filled the air with anti-Semitism and with hatred and with-- and it became so that we were practically-- all of a sudden, we were boycotted and ignored. And partially, of course, many of the boys that belonged to-- that were politically active and belonged to leftist parties were arrested. We had a young man who was a good friend of mine in our business, who happened to be as a young idealistic communist. And I never talked politics with him. We worked together in my father's business. And right after January 1933, he was arrested, taken to concentration camp. And I can still see him for me. He was not arrested in the-- in the office. He was picked up in the morning at home. And about a week later, his father came to tell my father that he was asked to pick up the ashes of his son in the concentration camp. And the favorite excuse was saying they were shot while trying to flee. None of them ever tried to escape. But they were shot. And immediately their bodies were burned. And the family's were notified to pick up the ashes. Naturally, from then on, our life was a living hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do You remember the name of that person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Herich Ganes. Herich Ganes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was taken to Dachau, which was not too far from Nuremberg, where the business was at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were brought up to work in this-- your future was going to be to work in the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the family business. I would have been the fourth generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: To backtrack a little bit, to the First World War, what part did your family play--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the First World War?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did play all the normal part that all the German Jews played in the war. They were, first of all, very patriotic. They were convinced that Germany fought a rightful war. And so my father, his brother, my mother's brothers, my mother's three brothers were all in the army, mostly in the front lines. My uncle-- my father's brother was an officer. And they were both on the west and the east front of Germany, in France and in Russia. And during that time, the business was totally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] neglected, practically non-existent, because there was nobody there but a few employees that could run it. But there was no merchandise to sell anyway. And I lived alone with my mother for four years, which I remember as a very deprived time. We had very little to eat. And we really-- the war had an enormous effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish question at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only-- the only question came up, and I never had any experience about that, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the war seemed to be lost, they were looking for somebody to blame. And it is always that the Jews were the natural source of blame. So they-- somebody had suggested to the Kaiser they should start counting how many Jews really served in the Army, whether they did their part. And this was rejected by the Kaiser's-- the Kaiser rejected it, and it was rejected also by some other advisers, because they found out that the Jews had served in the Army as a larger percentage than their percentage of the population. There was so many Jews in the army. And take 12,000 out of 500,000 were killed, which was a larger percentage than that of the total population. 12,000 Jewish men died at the front. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was the First World War. I remember the First World War. It's so long ago now it's, practically history. But I remember it as it happened, because with all that the deprivation and all the spirit of victory that began first and the sad ending, and the many, many news of dead in every family. In my own family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- my father had an uncle who lost three sons out of five. So that was a big sacrifice.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] You said that by 1933 things turn into a living hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen so fast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been underlying-- I'm sure that underlying anti-Semitism was there. But what also made it so fast was that the tremendous power that the Nazis exerted once they took over. The threat was so great that everybody buckled under. People did not resist. First of all, they really thought that the movement in the beginning would be beneficial for the people. And that anti-Semitism was only perhaps a minor feature of the entire rising of the German people to new freedom, a new recognition, and more equal life. The enthusiasm in the beginning was very, very great. But at the same time, the-- nobody dared to deviate. There were so few people that deviated that-- that felt-- that saw what was coming and that understood it. And the reason was that even the best friends of my parents' families that were friends of-- my father had friends from his early youth, with which-- boys with whom he played. They were Gentile Germans. And some of them came to him and said you must understand, we can no longer associate with you. We have to regard our children. If we do, we would be seen as Jew friends or whatever they would call us. And it would [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] affect the careers of our children. of our-- and that was the pressure that was exerted. And it was partly-- it was mostly-- it was more pressure than authentic wish to do-- to do all these terrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father and mother discussing this with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could not help-- I couldn't help overhearing it. In fact, I was then a young man in my parents' house. And the radio was the big source of-- in those days was the big source. There was no television, of course. The radio was the big source of entertainment and information. And my father, who was a patriotic German Jew, couldn't stand it that I even could leave the radio on to hear these speeches of Hitler and Goering and Goebbels. And I had to-- to do it low key in my room, so that my parents wouldn't be disturbed. It was too much for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they felt that this would pass over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They thought it was a passing fancy, something that would pass over. They didn't think it would take so many years to pass over and in such a horrible way. But they saw from the beginning that this was not the way that the country of Goethe and Beethoven and Schiller and Schopenhauer could end its history, with these-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these horrendous extremes and brutalities and totally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] totally inhuman actions.

speaker: interviewer
text: If your parents thought that this might be temporary, and that it might blow over, what were your feelings about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My feelings were more skeptical. I also hoped that it would eventually come to an end. I always knew, even at the time when Hitler was already at war with the United States and with half the world, that he would come down. And it looked like he was winning. And there were many skeptical people, even in this country, who thought the Nazis are winning. They're winning. It looked like they're winning. I always knew in my heart that they must lose. But the reason why I emigrated and left Germany is that the general feeling of the older generation, like my parents, was get the kids out. They will leave us alone. But for the young people, there is no future. There is no way to make a living. There is no way to make a career. It's over. So they have to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go any further there, you mentioned that during school you belonged to a fraternity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were Gentiles and Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had wonderful times together. Did any of those fraternity brothers make themselves known to you around 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, absolutely not. But that might also have been my reluctance to put them into a -- to put them into jeopardy. Because by the time, I lived no longer in Fuerth. The family had moved to Munich because we believed, erroneously, that the bigger city and the more international city, the university-- large university city with many foreigners living there would be a safer place for us than Fuerth, where close to Nuremberg, which was the birthplace of Nazism and had this terrible Julius Streicher, who was the [INAUDIBLE] of Nuremberg. [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay, we're back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family decided to move from Fuerth to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we felt that Munich was the big city, much, much bigger, also more international. It had many students and visitors from foreign countries. And people generally felt more freer there, that the Nazis were a little bit more cautious about what they did, at least at 1934, yet. I'm only talking about 1934, which was the beginning. And, of course, it was a mistake, a big financial mistake, because we spent an enormous amount of money to move our business from-- from Nuremberg to Munich. And we even paid the moves of our employees, of which most of them followed us to Munich. And that was only for a few years, when it got just as bad there, because after 1938, after the Kristallnacht, the business had to be sold, which means 'sold' in quotation marks. It was taken over by an Aryan firm for very little money. And actually only a token, but there was no choice. It had to be. We were forced to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you did make the move, when you moved the business to Munich, how many families did you move with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there must have been about 10 families, at least 10 families, that moved with us. And some of them decided not to move. They were actually the smart ones, because they rather refused to go, because it was only for a short time. But others may have been glad that they lived in Munich, which was-- was even during the war a better atmosphere than Nuremberg, Fuerth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in a new city, in Munich, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the life in Munich, I was only there from 1934 to 1936, when I went to America. These were two fairly good years-- we-- fairly good because we began to have our own cultural life. The Jewish community began to get itself together. We had our-- we were not allowed to go to any movies, any concerts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had our own concerts, our own lectures, our own possible celebrations and dances. And most lived in the family. Those who were not actually arrested and brought to concentration camps or to prison for trumped up charges, we lived in steady fear. It was a life that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a life under siege. These are the years 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933 Until I left in 1936. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was also the year of the Winter Olympics in 1936. And the Winter Olympics were close to Munich in Garmisch. And the Nazis tried to put up a front as if they were not as bad as they were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as it was advertised all over the world that they were. So they put up this front that made Munich a little bit of a-- a haven in some respects.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you attend the Olympics in 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must confess that I did. I was not supposed to. I was a little cheeky. I mean, I-- I went there by myself. And I should-- we were not supposed to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Jews you were not supposed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But who would know whether I was Jewish, Italian or French, because looks at an Olympic game are really no longer a-- a sure criterion. My dark hair, my complexion, my Jewish looks-- I could have been an Arab, for all that anybody knew. So I felt safe, and I was interested and curious, and I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a very-- it was a snowy day. And I drove a little Opal car. And on the way back, I had the misfortune to rear end another car in front of me. And it really-- my heart sank. What is going to happen to me if the police comes and this and that, and that I will be found guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the guy jumped out from the car that I rear ended. And he said, I want to introduce myself to you right away. My name is Jerome Cohen. So he was from Paris. He spoke with a French accent. He was a French reporter by the name of Jerome Cohen. So I knew Cohen would not hurt me. That was the outcome of my visit to the Olympics.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do you know which event-- which event did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't exactly remember. It was the-- It was an ice skating event. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't see as many events, really, as I mingled with the people. And we were so hungry to get out of our ghetto existence. I think that was really what prompted me to do this, that-- that it was just-- I felt good in just being amongst normal people who-- who were not marked people, who were not ghettoized.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you ghettoized in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, and everywhere, we really ghettoized in the fact that we couldn't go to the park. There were all over signs-- Jews are not wanted here. When we went-- there was a very small selected places, a few places-- I was still traveling for my father's business at that time. There were very few places where we could go to hotels. They had signs out. Jews are not wanted here. If you came, you were subject to prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have false papers of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. No, I had no papers. I don't remember that-- while I was still in Germany, our passports that we had previously were taken away from us. We had to hand in our passport so that we could not leave the country without getting our passports back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had no papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were ghettoized in every way that the--
